regions: Michigan: Detroit, Flint, Pontiac, Saginaw, Lansing, Dearborn
Other: St. Louis MO, Kenosha WI, Janesville WI, Framingham MA, Los Angeles CA

what_they_did.daily_work: Built automobiles on the assembly line — the defining industrial process of the 20th century. Henry Ford's Highland Park plant introduced the moving assembly line in 1913, and the method spread throughout the industry. Each worker performed one repetitive operation as the chassis moved past on a conveyor: installing fenders, tightening bolts, wiring ignition systems, hanging doors, painting, or upholstering. In foundry and stamping operations, workers poured molten metal and operated massive presses that stamped body panels from sheet steel.
what_they_did.seasonal_cycle: Model year changeover created a distinctive cycle. Plants typically shut down for 4-8 weeks in summer (June-August) for retooling, then ramped up production for the new model year in fall. This annual layoff was a major source of economic insecurity before Supplemental Unemployment Benefits (SUB pay) were negotiated in the 1950s.
tools_and_equipment: Air-powered impact wrenches and nut runners
what_they_did.typical_hours: Ford originally mandated a 48-hour week (6 eight-hour days). He cut to the 40-hour week (5 eight-hour days) in 1926. After the UAW organized, 40 hours was standard with overtime at time-and-a-half. During WWII, 48-54 hour weeks were common to meet military production demands.

economics.typical_arrangement: Paid hourly with a defined wage scale after UAW contracts (1937 onward). Before unionization, wages were set unilaterally by the company. Ford's announcement of the $5 day in January 1914 was revolutionary — it more than doubled the prevailing factory wage and triggered a mass migration to Detroit.
economics.annual_income_range: Pre-Ford $5 day: $2.00-$2.50/day ($600-$750/year). Ford $5 day (1914): $1,300-$1,500/year. 1920s: $6-$8/day. Post-UAW 1950s: $2.00-$2.50/hour base ($4,200-$5,200/year), plus overtime. 1970s: $5.00-$8.00/hour ($10,400-$16,640/year), plus cost-of-living adjustments, health benefits, and pensions. Auto wages were the gold standard of American blue-collar employment.
economics.comparison: Ford's $5 day in 1914 was more than double what a Southern sharecropper cleared in a year and triple what a Southern domestic servant earned. This wage differential — announced in newspapers across the country — was a direct trigger for the Great Migration to Detroit.
economics.debt_cycle: Not applicable. Auto workers earned high cash wages, particularly after unionization. The economic vulnerability was cyclical layoffs during model changeovers and recessions, not debt.

hazards: Repetitive motion injuries from performing the same operation thousands of times per shift
Noise-induced hearing loss from stamping presses and pneumatic tools
Lead poisoning in paint departments (before lead-free paint)
Burns and crush injuries in foundry operations
Chemical exposure to solvents, adhesives, and coolants
Speed-up — the relentless acceleration of the assembly line was the central grievance of auto workers
working_conditions.living_conditions: Auto workers lived in urban neighborhoods near the plants — Hamtramck, River Rouge, Flint's North End. Black auto workers were confined by racial covenants to specific neighborhoods (Paradise Valley and Black Bottom in Detroit). Housing conditions ranged from overcrowded rooming houses for new arrivals to modest single-family homes for established workers. By the 1950s-1960s, many white auto workers moved to suburban tract housing.
working_conditions.family_involvement: Auto plants employed some women during WWII ('Rosie the Riveter'), but peacetime assembly work was overwhelmingly male. Women re-entered auto plants in larger numbers in the 1960s-1970s. Auto worker wages were high enough for single-earner families, though many wives worked in service jobs to supplement income.
working_conditions.autonomy: Minimal on the line — the pace was dictated by the conveyor speed, and every motion was time-studied. The UAW won protections against arbitrary discipline, established grievance procedures, and negotiated production standards, but the fundamental character of assembly line work — repetitive, exhausting, mind-numbing — remained. Workers described it as 'selling your body and renting your mind.'

push_factors: Plant closures as manufacturers moved production to the South, Mexico, and overseas
Automation and robotics reduced assembly jobs (especially after 1980)
Import competition from Japanese and German automakers
Recessions of 1974-75 and 1980-82 devastated the industry
why_they_left.what_replaced_them: Robotic welding and painting, offshore production in Mexico (maquiladoras), Japanese transplant factories in non-union Southern states (Tennessee, Kentucky, Alabama), and overall industry contraction. UAW membership fell from 1.5 million in 1979 to under 400,000 by 2009.
why_they_left.narrative_hook: The Ford hiring office that {ancestor_name} walked into when arriving in {destination} from {county} around {year} was probably the most consequential door the family ever walked through — on the other side was a wage that could change everything.

records_generated.what_it_shows: Name, hire date, badge number, department, job classification, wage rate, home address, emergency contact
records_generated.where_to_find: Benson Ford Research Center (Ford Motor Company archives, Dearborn MI), General Motors Heritage Center, Walter P. Reuther Library (Wayne State University, Detroit), Kettering University Archives (Flint)
records_generated.research_tip: Ford Motor Company kept remarkably detailed employee records from the earliest years. The Benson Ford Research Center will search their records by name for genealogical inquiries — start here for any Ford ancestor.

records_generated.what_it_shows: Membership cards, dues records, seniority lists, grievance files, local union meeting minutes
records_generated.where_to_find: Walter P. Reuther Library at Wayne State University (the primary archive for UAW records)
records_generated.research_tip: UAW Local union records can establish when a worker arrived, which plant and department they worked in, and their progression through the seniority system. Grievance files can reveal individual workplace experiences.

records_generated.record_type: City directories (Detroit, Flint, and other auto cities)
records_generated.what_it_shows: Name, home address, employer, occupation — published annually
records_generated.where_to_find: Detroit Public Library Burton Historical Collection, Flint Public Library, Ancestry.com digitized collections
records_generated.research_tip: Annual city directories allow year-by-year tracking of when a family arrived in an auto city and where they lived relative to the plants. Cross-reference with reverse directories that list addresses to see who their neighbors were.

census_clues.what_to_look_for: A family from the rural South, Appalachia, or Eastern Europe appearing in Detroit, Flint, or another auto city between 1914-1950. If the census lists the specific company (Ford, GM, Chrysler, Buick), this is highly valuable for locating employment records. Look at neighboring households — if multiple neighbors work at the same plant, the neighborhood is an auto worker community.
census_clues.red_flags: A Southern Black family appearing in Detroit between 1916-1930 with the head listed as an auto worker is a textbook Great Migration family. If they are in Hamtramck or Highland Park, they were likely at Ford. If in Flint, likely at Buick or Chevrolet.

cultural_context: The automobile industry did not merely employ millions of workers — it created the modern American working class. Ford's $5 day, the UAW sit-down strikes of 1936-37, the Treaty of Detroit (1950 GM-UAW contract that established the template for postwar labor relations), and the deindustrialization of the 1970s-1980s are all defining chapters of American history. For Black migrants from the South, the auto plant was the gateway to the middle class — and its closure was a devastating blow to communities that had been built around the industry.

counter_narratives.claim: Auto work was not limited to the assembly line
counter_narratives.context: The auto industry employed workers in enormous variety: tool-and-die makers (the aristocracy of the trade), pattern makers, electricians, millwrights, janitors, truck drivers, office clerks, engineers, and managers. A major auto complex like Ford's Rouge plant was essentially a self-contained industrial city with its own power plant, steel mill, glass factory, and rubber works.

counter_narratives.claim: Ford's $5 day was not pure generosity
counter_narratives.context: Ford's $5 day (1914) came with strings attached. Workers had to submit to investigation by the Ford Sociological Department, which inspected their homes, verified their family status, and judged their 'habits' — including sobriety, cleanliness, and whether they spoke English. Immigrant workers who failed the inspection received only the base wage ($2.34/day) until they 'improved.' This paternalism was part of Ford's broader vision of social control.

source_references.author: Sugrue, Thomas J.
source_references.title: The Origins of the Urban Crisis: Race and Inequality in Postwar Detroit
source_references.notes: Pulitzer Prize finalist that traces how deindustrialization and racial discrimination together produced Detroit's decline — essential for understanding the post-1950 auto worker experience

source_references.author: Lichtenstein, Nelson
source_references.title: The Most Dangerous Man in Detroit: Walter Reuther and the Fate of American Labor
source_references.notes: Biography of the UAW president that doubles as a comprehensive history of the auto industry's labor relations

source_references.author: Boyle, Kevin
source_references.title: Arc of Justice: A Saga of Race, Civil Rights, and Murder in the Jazz Age
source_references.notes: National Book Award winner examining the Black auto worker experience in 1920s Detroit through the Ossian Sweet case

source_references.author: Meyer, Stephen
source_references.title: The Five Dollar Day: Labor Management and Social Control in the Ford Motor Company, 1908-1921
source_references.notes: Definitive study of Ford's revolutionary wage policy and the Sociological Department that enforced it
